name: Poem: "The House on Elm Street" — Naida Bush

sourceRefs.label: civilserviceaspirants.in - two dedicated MCQ sets (12 questions total), Group 2/2A specific

researchNotes: OCR index search for 'house on elm street/naida bush' returned zero hits — no past-paper evidence, web research only (matches the pattern of several other less-tested Unit VII poems). NOTE: the source site itself spells the author 'Nadia Bush' in its own question text while the TNPSC syllabus PDF spells it 'Naida Bush' — a name-spelling discrepancy similar to other name-confusion traps already flagged elsewhere in this project; using the syllabus's official spelling 'Naida Bush' as authoritative, but flagging the variant since a real exam's options may use either spelling. No full poem text or textbook source was available locally to verify the literary analysis directly (same limitation as other poems with no local English-medium textbook) — content below is reconstructed entirely from the two coaching-site MCQ sets' question+answer pairs, all of which are internally consistent with each other on theme/imagery. Expanded 2026-07 pass: added a small amount of additional synthesis (overall mood-building technique, significance of the unresolved ending) staying strictly within what's supported by the existing verified facts — deliberately did NOT assume any connection to the 'A Nightmare on Elm Street' film franchise, since no sourced material confirms this beyond the coincidental street name, and fabricating that link would be exactly the kind of unverified claim this project avoids.

reading.intro: A poem describing an isolated, eerie, possibly haunted house, told through a narrator who feels both curiosity and fear. Real coaching-site evidence focuses on (a) the house's physical description and atmosphere, (b) the narrator's emotional reaction, and (c) figures of speech describing the house as if alive.

qa.question: Who wrote "The House on Elm Street", and what is the poem's central theme?
qa.answer: Naida Bush (spelled 'Nadia Bush' on some coaching sites — syllabus spelling 'Naida Bush' is authoritative). Central theme: a mysterious, isolated house that feels eerie and almost alive, exploring how the unknown provokes a mix of curiosity and fear in an observer.

qa.question: How is the house's isolation and emptiness described?
qa.answer: "It sat alone" — describing the house as lonely and isolated, set apart from other buildings. "Bare to the bone" describes it as completely empty or lifeless, stripped of any warmth or occupants.

qa.question: What dominant emotion does the poet/narrator express toward the house?
qa.answer: Curiosity mixed with fear — the narrator is drawn to investigate the mysterious house but is simultaneously afraid, and per the poem, fear ultimately overtakes curiosity (the narrator does not enter).

qa.question: What unnatural/spooky details does the poem use to bring the house 'to life'?
qa.answer: Lights that flicker on and off (suggesting something unnatural rather than a simple power outage); the house seeming 'a bit brighter' in summer (around May); and a tree beside the house that strangely never grows or sheds its leaves — all small unnatural details that build an eerie atmosphere.

qa.question: What does the line "It plays with your mind" suggest?
qa.answer: Psychological/mental unease and confusion — the house's effect is described as primarily a mental or emotional disturbance rather than a physical threat.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "At night the house seems to be alive."
qa.answer: Personification — attributing life/animation to the inanimate house.

qa.question: Identify the figure of speech: "The house seems to be a bit brighter" (in the context of describing the house's mood/behaviour changing with the season).
qa.answer: Personification — again attributing a living, changeable quality (mood/appearance shifting like a living thing) to the house.

qa.question: How does the poem build its overall eerie mood?
qa.answer: Through the accumulation of several small, individually ambiguous unnatural details (flickering lights, an unchanging tree, seasonal mood shifts) rather than any single dramatic event — the unease builds gradually rather than through one clear scare.

qa.question: Why is it significant that the narrator does not enter the house by the poem's end?
qa.answer: It leaves the central mystery deliberately unresolved — the reader never learns for certain whether the house is truly haunted or the narrator's fear is unfounded, reinforcing the poem's theme that fear of the unknown can feel as powerful as any confirmed danger.

qa.question: What effect does pairing an ordinary setting (an 'Elm Street' house) with eerie qualities create?
qa.answer: It heightens the poem's uncanny effect — an otherwise unremarkable, everyday house becomes unsettling precisely because it seems almost, but not quite, normal.

questions.prompt: "The House on Elm Street" was written by:
questions.explanation: Naida Bush (per the TNPSC syllabus spelling; some sources spell it 'Nadia Bush').

questions.prompt: The central theme of the poem is:
What dominant emotion does the narrator express toward the house?
"It sat alone" implies that the house is:
The phrase "bare to the bone" (describing the house) means:
Why does the narrator ultimately not enter the house?
"Lights flicker on and off" in the poem creates an image of:
What is strange about the tree beside the house?
options: It never grows or sheds its leaves
questions.explanation: It never grows or sheds its leaves.

questions.prompt: "It plays with your mind" refers to:
"At night the house seems to be alive" is an example of which figure of speech?
The poem's overall eerie mood is built mainly through:
options: The accumulation of small, ambiguous unnatural details
Direct narration of a ghost's appearance
questions.explanation: The accumulation of small, individually ambiguous eerie details.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator's decision not to enter the house leave unresolved?
options: Whether the house is truly haunted or the fear is unfounded
questions.explanation: Whether the house is truly haunted or the fear is unfounded.

questions.prompt: Pairing the ordinary setting of "Elm Street" with the house's eerie qualities creates what effect?
